one_line_takeaway: A synthesis-style survey of the LLM-for-hardware-security literature that names cross-cutting failure patterns (accessibility-competence paradox, alignment paradox, non-transferable hyperparameter tuning, benchmark contamination) but contributes no new experiments, attacks, or defenses of its own.
key_finding: The paper's own analytical contribution is identifying several recurring cross-cutting tensions that individual siloed papers do not name as a class: (1) an 'accessibility-competence paradox' -- intermediate representations most accessible to non-hardware engineers (HLS-C, PyMTL3) have the worst LLM code-generation quality due to scarce training data, while harder-to-learn HDLs (Verilog, Chisel) perform best; (2) an 'alignment paradox' -- general-purpose LLM safety training simultaneously over-refuses legitimate hardware-security engineering requests and under-refuses semantically disguised adversarial ones; (3) hyperparameter/decoding configuration can matter as much as model choice or architecture for RTL-generation quality, and optimal configurations do not transfer across benchmarks; (4) static benchmarks in this domain are pervasively contaminated, motivating dynamic/feedback-driven benchmark generation as a structural fix rather than a one-off patch.
core_contribution: This is a survey/review paper (not primary research) by Knechtel, Sinanoglu, and Karri (NYU/NYU Abu Dhabi) that synthesizes the recent literature on LLMs applied to Electronic Design Automation (EDA) and hardware security. The paper's own contribution is the organization and cross-cutting synthesis itself: it structures ~49 cited works into five areas -- (II) LLM-driven hardware design (RTL synthesis, HLS, verification, model orchestration), (III) threats from LLM-driven design (backdoors, data contamination, jailbreaking/misalignment, IP leakage), (IV) LLM-driven design-for-security (logic locking, side-channel/PQC, Trojan detection, red-teaming, bug detection), and (V) education/CTF initiatives -- and distills seven 'lessons learned' (Agentic Iteration, Hyperparameters vs Architectures, Data Modality vs Capability, Representation Bottleneck, Alignment Paradox, Red-Teaming, Dynamic vs Static Benchmarking). No new experiments, datasets, models, or attacks are introduced by the authors of this paper itself.
threat_model: The paper surveys, rather than defines a single threat model of its own, several distinct threat models drawn from cited work: (1) data poisoning/backdoor insertion into fine-tuning corpora via rare trigger keywords (RTL-Breaker, cited ref [5]) causing an LLM to emit hardware Trojans or degraded logic (e.g., ripple-carry instead of carry-lookahead adders) while passing functional checks; (2) benchmark data contamination where test sets leak into pretraining data, inflating apparent capability (VeriContaminated, ref [6]); (3) jailbreaking/safety misalignment where general-purpose LLM guardrails over-refuse legitimate hardware-security requests yet are bypassed by semantically disguised prompts, e.g., framing a logic-locking attack as 'PPA-driven resynthesis' (HarmChip, ref [8]); (4) proprietary IP leakage from LLMs fine-tuned on in-house RTL, which can regenerate industrial cryptographic accelerators given minor structural hints (VeriLeaky, ref [4]); (5) adversarial evasion of ML-based hardware-security detectors (GNN piracy/Trojan detectors) via RL+LLM-orchestrated netlist rewriting or AST restructuring (NetDeTox ref [9], TrojanGYM ref [16]). Because this is a review paper, none of these threat models are original to the authors -- they are attributed to the cited primary-research papers.
limitations: As a survey, the paper carries the inherent limitations of the genre: it does not independently verify the cited quantitative claims (e.g., contamination rates, F1 scores, evasion rates) it reports, most cited sources are unpublished arXiv preprints or in-press/forthcoming venues (many dated 2026, several by overlapping author sets including the survey's own authors, raising self-citation density concerns), and there is no discussion of a systematic search/inclusion methodology (no PRISMA-style protocol, no stated criteria for which papers were included versus omitted). The review does not critically weigh conflicting results across cited works or discuss threats to the validity of the surveyed papers' own experiments.

quantitative_results: All quantitative figures below are reported BY THIS SURVEY as summaries of cited primary-research papers, not original results produced by this paper's authors.

Representation-bottleneck study (cited ref [7]): Verilog simulation pass rates of 83%-88% vs. HLS-C pass rates of only 3%-10%, evaluated across six IRs (Verilog, VHDL, Chisel, Bluespec, PyMTL3, HLS-C) over 202 tasks
VeriDispatcher (cited ref [26]): reduces costly API calls by 40% via pre-inference difficulty prediction routing

'Configuration Over Selection' study (cited ref [29]): sweeping 108 decoding configurations produced absolute pass-rate gaps of up to 25.5 percentage points between best and worst settings for the same model; Spearman rank correlation of configuration rankings across VerilogEval and RTLLM benchmarks was near zero
VeriContaminated (cited ref [6]): near-100% contamination rates found in standard benchmarks like VerilogEval across most commercial models (using Min-K% Prob and Cross-Document Distance metrics)
ARIANNA (cited ref [37]): reduces eFPGA redaction area overheads by up to 3.3x
NetDeTox (cited ref [9]): bypasses GNN-based piracy detectors across 90% of test cases, with some adversarial circuits achieving negative area overhead versus baseline
TrojanGYM (cited ref [16]): achieves 83% evasion rate against a robust GNN Trojan detector via iterative AST restructuring
TrojanLoC (cited ref [41]): 99% F1-score for module-level Trojan detection using an RTL-finetuned transformer plus gradient-boosting classifier on the TrojanInS dataset
VeriCWEty (cited ref [43]): 89% precision identifying CWE-1244/CWE-1245-type vulnerabilities and 96% accuracy at line-level bug localization
